a: A database is an organized place to store data so you can save it, find it, and change it reliably. Instead of loose files, it keeps your data in a structured way and a program called the database lets many users read and write it safely at the same time.
fs: A database is structured, durable storage that lets many users query and update the same data safely and consistently.
fj: Thinking a database is just one big spreadsheet or file, instead of a managed system built for safe shared access.

a: A primary key is a column whose value uniquely identifies each row in a table, like an id number. No two rows can share the same primary key, and it can never be empty, so you can always point to exactly one row.
fs: A primary key is the unique, non-null identifier for each row — one value points to exactly one record.
fj: Picking a name or email as the key when it can repeat or be blank; keys must be unique and never empty.

a: A foreign key is a column in one table that points to the primary key of another table, linking the two. For example, an orders table stores a customer_id that refers to a row in the customers table, so each order knows who it belongs to.
fs: A foreign key references another table's primary key, linking rows and enforcing that the reference actually exists.
fj: Assuming any column with a matching name links tables; it only links if defined as a foreign key to a real key.

a: An index is a lookup structure the database builds so it can find rows fast without scanning the whole table, like the index at the back of a book. It speeds up searches and filters on a column, but it uses extra space and slightly slows down writes.
fs: An index is a side structure that lets the database jump to matching rows instead of scanning every row.
fj: Believing more indexes are always better; each one costs storage and makes inserts and updates slower.

code.label: index a lookup column
lines: -- finds rows without a full scan

a: A transaction groups several database changes into one all-or-nothing unit. If every step succeeds you COMMIT and they all save together; if anything fails you ROLLBACK and none of them happen, so the data is never left half-changed.
fs: A transaction makes a group of changes atomic — they all commit together or all roll back, never partway.
fj: Thinking each statement saves on its own; until you COMMIT, the grouped changes can still be fully undone.
